<!-- Topic 4: Fill In the Code -->
<!-- Total slides: 3 -->

# Fill In the Missing Code

---

## Exercise: print an employee record {.smaller}

+ The struct and `main` are complete — the function body is missing.
+ Fill in lines 28–30 so the program compiles and prints both employee records.

::: notes
Total slides: 3
Students need to write three cout statements using dot notation. This is deliberately straightforward — the goal is fluency with `e.member` syntax, not problem-solving under pressure. About 5 minutes.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## The code — fill in lines 28–30 {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
using namespace std;

struct Employee {
    int    employeeID;
    string name;
    double rate;
};

void printEmployee(Employee e);

int main() {
    Employee a;
    a.employeeID = 14335;
    a.name       = "Pat Smith";
    a.rate       = 17.55;

    Employee b = {29401, "Jordan Lee", 19.75};

    printEmployee(a);
    printEmployee(b);

    return 0;
}

void printEmployee(Employee e) {
    // === YOUR CODE HERE (lines 28–30) ===
    // Print: "ID: [employeeID]"
    // Print: "Name: [name]"
    // Print: "Rate: $[rate]"
    // =====================================
}
```

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Solution {.smaller}

```{.cpp}
void printEmployee(Employee e) {
    cout << "ID: "    << e.employeeID << "\n";
    cout << "Name: "  << e.name       << "\n";
    cout << "Rate: $" << e.rate       << "\n";
}
```

::: notes
Output:
ID: 14335
Name: Pat Smith
Rate: $17.55
ID: 29401
Name: Jordan Lee
Rate: $19.75
:::

<!-- Slide 3 -->
